content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 30, 2010

Nancy C. Miller
Miller, Owen & Trost
428 J Street, Suite 400
Sacramento, California 92626-7670

RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-10-197

Dear Ms. Miller:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Calaveras Council of
Governments (the "Council") Boardmember Tom Tryon regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ and is based on the facts presented; the Fair
Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it
renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Additionally, we base our advice solely
on the provisions of the Act and do not address the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-
interest laws, such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090.

QUESTION

May Boardmember Tryon participate in the Council's decisions related to the California
Department of Transportation's Wagon Trail Project on Highway 4 in Calaveras County?

CONCLUSION

Yes. So long as the decision will not result in a reasonably foreseeable increase in gross
revenues of $20,000 or more for Boardmember Tryon's business, as explained below, he may
participate in the governmental decisions.

FACTS

The Council was formed in 1998 through a Joint Powers Agreement between Calaveras
County and the City of Angels and is the Regional Transportation Planning Agency for
Calaveras County. The Council's purpose is to act as the lead planning and administrative

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-10-197
Page No. 2

agency for Calaveras County's transportation projects and programs. The Council has seven
board members; two appointed by the Calaveras County Board of Supervisors, two appointed by
the Angels City Council, and three at large members of the public.

The Council is in the process of selecting a new route for Highway 4 to the west of the
City of Angels. This realignment is known as the "Wagon Trail Project." One of the
realignment routes could result in the unused portions of old Highway 4 being converted into a
trail for use by horses, hikers, and bicyclists.

Boardmember Tryon owns and operates a "Bed & Barn" business on the southeastern
edge of the City of Angels. His business caters to horse owners. The eastern edge of the Wagon
Trail Project is approximately 5.5 miles west of the subject business.²

POTENTIAL CONFLICT OF INTEREST

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) Section 87100
prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest.

The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The general rule,
however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a governmental
decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his or her
financial interests.

STEPS 1 & 2: IS BOARDMEMBER TRYON A PUBLIC OFFICIAL MAKING, PARTICIPATING IN
MAKING, OR INFLUENCING A GOVERNMENTAL DECISION?

As a member of the Council, Boardmember Tryon is a public official under the Act.
(Section 82048.) Consequently, he may not make, participate in making, or otherwise use his
official position to influence any decisions that will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on any of his economic interests. Because Boardmember Tryon will be called
upon to consider proposed projects before the Council, he will be making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his official position to influence a governmental decision.³

² We assume this is the closest point to Boardmember Tryon's property.

³ If a public official's office is listed in section 87200 ("87200 filers" include planning commission
members) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: (1)
immediately prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identify each type of economic interest involved in the
decision as well as details of the economic interest, as discussed in regulation 18702.5(b)(1)(B), on the record of the
meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the
item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal
interests, special rules found in regulation 18702.5, subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.)

File No. A-10-197
Page No. 3

STEP 3: DOES BOARDMEMBER TRYON HAVE A POTENTIALLY DISQUALIFYING ECONOMIC
INTEREST?

A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section
87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generality, on the official, a member of his or her
immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic interests, including:

• An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or
indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a));
or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any
position of management. (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b).)

• An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.)

• An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income,
aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.)

• An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to
$420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e);
Regulation 18703.4.)

• An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family — this is the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103;
Regulation 18703.5.)

You have indicated that Boardmember Tryon owns a "Bed & Barn" business. For
purposes of our analysis we assume that Boardmember Tryon's investment in the business is at
least $2,000 and that he owns the property and it is valued at $2,000 or more. He therefore has
an economic interest in both the business and the real property. Additionally, Boardmember
Tryon also has a source of income economc interest in the business and, because he owns the
business, an economic interest in any client who is a source of income to the business
aggregating $500 or more within six months before any governmental decision.¹

You have not provided any information with respect to any income from guests of the
business or any other real property, business or other potential economic interests. Accordingly,
our analysis is limited to Boardmember Tryon's economic interests in the real property on which
the Bed & Barn is located and in that business entity.

¹ According to the website www.tryonranch.com, the rates begin at $300 per night (plus an additional $100
if bringing horses) and a minimum two-day stay is required.

File No. A-10-197
Page No. 4

STEP 4: IS THE ECONOMIC INTEREST DIRECTLY OR INDIRECTLY INVOLVED IN THE
GOVERNMENTAL DECISION?5

"In order to determine if a governmental decision's reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on a given economic interest is material, it must first be determined if the official's
economic interest is directly involved or indirectly involved in the governmental decision."
(Regulation 18704(a).) For governmental decisions that affect real property interests, the
standards set forth in regulation 18704.2 apply. For governmental decisions that affect business entities, the standards set forth in regulations 18704.1 apply.
(Regulation 18704(a)(1).)

For real property economic interests, Regulation 18704.2(a)(1) states:

"(a) Real property in which a public official has an economic interest is
directly involved in a governmental decision if any of the following apply:

"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of
that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the property [that] is the subject of the governmental decision."

You have indicated that the property on which the business is located is more than 500
feet from the property that will be the subject of the governmental decisions. Therefore, because
the trail is located more than 500 feet from his business property, and there are no other factors
that apply under the regulation that would indicate that the property is directly in governmental
decisions, the property is indirectly involved.

For business entities and sources of income, Regulation 18704.1 states that the business
entity or source-of-income is directly involved when it either:

"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;

"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the
subject person."

Because Boardmember Tryon's business and source-of-income does not fall into either of
the above two classifications, these economic interests are also indirectly involved in the
governmental decisions.

5 If a public official's economic interest in not directly involved in a governmental decision, it is considered
"indirectly involved." (Regulation 18704(a).)

File No. A-10-197
Page No. 5


STEP 5: MATERIALITY STANDARD

A conflict of interest arises only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material. (Regulation
18700(a).)

For indirectly involved real property, Regulation 18705.2(b)(1) provides that the
financial effect of a governmental decision on real property is presumed not to be material.

". . . This presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are specific
circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its financial effect, and the
nature of the real property in which the public official has an economic interest,
which make it reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on the real property in which the public official has an interest."
(See Regulation 18705.2(b)(1)(A-C), copy enclosed, for examples of specific
circumstances that may rebut the presumption.)

You have not provided any information indicating any circumstances that may
rebut the presumption.

For business entities and sources-of-income that are indirectly involved, the materiality
standards are provided in Regulation 18705.1(c). For a relatively small business, the standards
in subdivision (c)(4) apply, and the financial effect of the governmental decision on the business
entity is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that :

"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or more;
or,

"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring or
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal
year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,

"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more."

STEP 6: REASONABLY FORESEEABLE

Whether the financial consequences of a governmental decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time the decision is made depends on the facts surrounding the decision. A
financial effect need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more
than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

File No. A-10-197
Page No. 6

Ultimately, it is up to the public official to make the determination through a good faith
effort. (Hensley Advice Letter, No. A-07-113; Moock Advice Letter, No. A-01-140; O'Harra
Advice Letter, No. A-00-174.) However, given the above standards, unless the new riding trail,
which you have indicated is 5.5 miles away on the other side of a mountain, is such an attraction
to potential guests so as to reasonably foreseeably increase reservations at the "Tryon Ranch Bed
& Barn Vacation Home"⁶ that gross revenue will increase by at least $20,000 in a fiscal year,
Boardmember Tryon does not have a conflict of interest under the Act.

STEPS 7 AND 8: PUBLIC GENERALLY AND LEGALLY REQUIRED PARTICIPATION

You have not given any facts indicating that either the "public generally" exception or
that the "legally required participation" exception would apply.

If you have any questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: William J Lenkeit
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

WJL:jgl

⁶ Website description.

CONCLUSION: Yes. So long as the decision will not result in a reasonably foreseeable increase in gross
revenlles of $20,000 or more for Boardmember Tryon's business, as explained below, he may
participate in the governmental decisions.
